meta.name: Winecellar Design System
meta.purpose: Authoritative UI design guide for humans and AI building new features in this application.
meta.designIntent: Create a premium, warm, structured product experience that feels like a modern cellar journal: refined, tactile, trustworthy, and calm.

brand.coreMetaphor: A private wine cellar translated into software

creativeDirection.styleSummary: Editorial luxury grounded in utility. Interfaces should blend the warmth of wine culture with the clarity of a professional inventory tool.
experienceGoals: Make bottle and cellar data feel valuable and carefully kept.
Keep forms and workflows clear, fast, and low-friction.
Use strong hierarchy and restrained ornament rather than decorative overload.
Make empty states aspirational, not barren.
Communicate trust for authentication, inventory, and audit history.

designPrinciples: Lead with atmosphere, then structure. Pages should feel premium at first glance and obvious to use at second glance.
Use dark, wine-forward tones carefully. Most product surfaces should remain light or softly tinted for readability.
Treat data like a collection catalog. Alignment, spacing, and labels must feel deliberate.
Make primary actions unmistakable, but never loud enough to cheapen the interface.
Prefer fewer, stronger accents. One rich color and one metallic highlight are enough.
Use card grouping and section dividers to mirror cellar compartments and bottle rows.
Show hierarchy through typography, spacing, and material contrast before using extra borders.
Reward deeper product moments with richer visuals, but keep auth and utility flows clean and focused.

usageRules: Default pages use warm light neutrals, not stark white.
Reserve brandPrimary for primary actions, active navigation, key highlights, and critical emphasis.
Use brandSecondary sparingly as a premium metallic accent for badges, key numbers, and decorative dividers.
Dark backgrounds should appear in hero sections, modal emphasis states, or selected-cellar headers, not as the global default.
Semantic colors must always pair with tinted backgrounds in status surfaces.
Never place large bodies of text on brandPrimary backgrounds unless the text is inverse and spacing is generous.

typography.fallbackStrategy: If the preferred fonts are unavailable, keep a high-contrast serif for headings and a clean contemporary sans-serif for UI/body copy.
rules: Use the serif display face for hero headlines, page-opening moments, and premium data callouts only.
Use the sans-serif family for navigation, forms, tables, cards, and all interactive UI.
Labels and overlines may use uppercase with generous tracking; body copy and buttons should remain in sentence case.
Avoid fully justified text.
Keep line lengths between 45 and 80 characters for paragraph content.

layoutRules: Card padding: 20px compact cards, 24px default cards, 32px feature cards.
Section spacing should expand significantly between page bands to preserve a calm premium rhythm.
Dense data areas may reduce vertical spacing, but should never feel cramped.

guidance: Prefer subtle warm borders over cool gray lines.
Use stronger borders only for selected states, table delineation, and high-importance containers.
Do not stack border emphasis and strong shadows on the same component unless the component is modal.

rules: Shadows should feel like soft architectural depth, not floating neon cards.
Use shadows more on modals, dropdowns, and hero cards than on every small control.
On mobile, reduce shadow blur slightly to keep surfaces crisp.

patterns: Use fade and rise for dialogs, drawers, and flyouts.
Use subtle background tint shifts on hover rather than dramatic movement.
Primary buttons may lift by 1-2px on hover on desktop only.
Avoid bouncing, elastic motion, or overscaled transitions.

iconography.style: Clean outlined icons with slight human softness rather than purely geometric tech icons.

rules: Landing pages can intentionally break the grid with overlapping editorial blocks, but content edges must still align to base gutters.
Application pages should use predictable column spans for scannability.
Data tables and list rows should align to consistent internal column systems.

lightMode.description: Primary operating mode for all product surfaces.

darkMode.description: Optional premium mode for selected experiences, but parity should follow light mode design decisions rather than redefine them.
guidelines: Use deep espresso and aubergine surfaces instead of pure black.
Keep gold accents muted and matte.
Do not invert every semantic color literally; retune them for contrast.

pageBackgrounds.marketing: Warm parchment gradient or softly textured neutral field
pageBackgrounds.application: Flat warm canvas with occasional tinted sections
pageBackgrounds.modalBackdrop: Dark translucent overlay with slight blur if supported

materials.name: parchment
materials.use: landing hero backgrounds, empty states, editorial callouts
materials.description: Soft warm neutral with low-contrast tonal variation

materials.name: stone-card
materials.description: Light raised surface with warm border and restrained shadow

materials.name: reserve-dark
materials.use: hero bands, selected cellar header, premium summary tiles
materials.description: Deep cellar-toned surface with inverse typography

microcopyRules: Use straightforward product language before wine jargon.
When wine terminology appears, keep it accurate and restrained.
Buttons should use explicit verbs: Sign in, Create cellar, Save bottle, Invite member.
Empty states should suggest progress and collection-building, not failure.
Validation errors should be plain and corrective, not dramatic.

startPage.goal: Explain the product quickly and establish a premium first impression.
startPage.layout: Editorial hero with strong serif headline, supporting proof blocks, feature grid, and clear sign-in/register actions.
visualNotes: Use one dramatic wine-related image or atmospheric cellar composition, not a collage.
Allow generous whitespace around the hero copy.
Keep CTAs visible above the fold on desktop and mobile.

registrationPage.goal: Make account creation feel secure, elegant, and low-friction.
registrationPage.layout: Single focused form card with optional side panel for brand storytelling on desktop.
rules: Prioritize form clarity over decoration.
Use progress reassurance for activation-email step.
Place terms, privacy, and support links in quiet supporting text.

signInPage.goal: Provide a fast trusted re-entry point.
signInPage.layout: Compact centered auth card or split layout aligned with registration styling.
rules: Keep password recovery and registration links visible but secondary.
Error states must remain calm and readable.

cellarsPage.goal: Orient signed-in users and expose their cellar universe clearly.
cellarsPage.layout: Header summary followed by cellar cards or an aspirational empty state.
rules: Use stronger structure than on marketing pages.
Cellar cards should show name, role, bottle count if available, and recent activity hints.
Empty state should include a strong create/join path.

dataPages.goal: Make dense inventory information feel organized and premium.
patterns: Sticky page header with contextual actions
Detail panel or dedicated detail page

globalNav.style: Light surface or translucent warm surface with subtle bottom border
globalNav.logoTreatment: Wordmark or mark should feel understated and premium; avoid oversized app-logo treatment.
globalNav.activeState: BrandPrimary text with short underline or pill tint
globalNav.mobileBehavior: Use a sheet or drawer, not a full-screen novelty menu

secondaryNav.style: Tabs or segmented pills depending on density
secondaryNav.usage: Cellar-level navigation, settings sub-sections, inventory views

breadcrumbs.style: Small muted text with medium-weight current item
breadcrumbs.usage: Deep cellar flows such as bottle detail, compartment detail, settings

iconButton.style: Rounded-square or circular depending on context; default to rounded-square for app utility.

searchField.style: TextField with leading search icon and slightly more pill-like silhouette for top-level search

featureCard.mediaTreatment: Large image or illustration with warm overlay if needed

cellarCard.layout: Title, role/meta row, stats, and primary action area
cellarCard.accent: Thin top border or badge in brandSecondary or brandPrimary

bottleCard.layout: Vintage, producer, wine name, region, storage location, quantity
bottleCard.visualAnchor: Bottle silhouette, label preview, or restrained color band

rules: Alternate row fills only when density is high; otherwise use whitespace and dividers.
Selection states should use brandPrimarySoft background and stronger text weight.
Important numeric columns should align right.
Use tables for inventory only when users need to scan multiple attributes quickly.
Keep action columns compact and right-aligned.
Never overload a single row with more than two action buttons.

emptyStates.layout: Illustration or atmospheric object, direct headline, explanation, and one primary action
doNot: Fill space with generic stock icons

tile.valueStyle: serif heading or strong sans numeric display depending on context

activityTimeline.style: Vertical timeline with restrained markers and warm dividers
activityTimeline.timestampStyle: small muted mono or meta style

toolbar.layout: Search first, filters second, sort and view controls aligned opposite
toolbar.background: transparent or surface card depending on density

dataVisualization.usage: Minimal and functional; charts should support cellar understanding, not dominate the interface.
rules: Prefer bars, stacked bars, and compact line charts over novelty chart types.
Use warm neutral grids and axes.
Annotate important values directly when possible.

artDirection: Use cellar architecture, bottle labels, cork texture, glass reflections, and vineyard detail sparingly and with restraint.
Favor moody natural light or soft daylight over heavily stylized neon photography.
Crop images to preserve negative space for text overlays where needed.
imagery.illustrationStyle: Minimal editorial line art or softly shaded forms inspired by bottle shapes, racks, maps, and tags.
imagery.texturePolicy: Subtle texture is allowed in large backgrounds only; never under dense text or data tables.

rules: Stack editorial split layouts into a single column on mobile while preserving strong hierarchy.
Collapse dense tables into card lists or horizontally scrollable containers only when unavoidable.
Primary actions should remain thumb-reachable on mobile forms.
Navigation and contextual actions should never compete for the same top-row space on small screens.
Do not rely on wine-red hues alone to communicate state or selection.
Every input must have a persistent visible label.
Focus states must be clearly visible on light and dark surfaces.
Touch targets should be at least 44x44px.
Motion should respect reduced-motion preferences with shortened or removed non-essential animation.

mustDo: Always apply the warm-neutral foundation before introducing feature-specific styling.
Use the serif display font only for emphasis moments; default interactive UI to the sans-serif family.
Preserve consistent component radii, border colors, and button heights across screens.
When inventing new components, build from existing card, input, badge, and toolbar patterns.
Keep marketing pages more atmospheric and product pages more structured.
Use terminology from the sitemap and product spec exactly.
mustNotDo: Do not switch to generic SaaS blue as the primary accent.
Do not use purple gradients, black-glass dashboards, or crypto-style glow effects.
Do not mix more than one decorative serif or more than two UI font families.
Do not design dense enterprise tables without warm spacing and hierarchy.
Do not add playful illustrations or mascot-like graphics.
newFeatureChecklist: Identify whether the screen is marketing, authentication, navigation, inventory, detail, or settings oriented.
Choose the closest page template and component family from this file.
Apply foundation tokens first: color, type, spacing, radius, border, shadow.
Confirm primary and secondary actions are obvious and use approved button patterns.
Ensure empty, loading, error, and success states match the tone rules.
Verify mobile and desktop layouts both preserve the premium cellar feel.
